content:
Data Loader Guide Overview data-loader (entry point for cli/data_loader.py) is the unified command for fetching and inspecting ocean data from multiple sources:
Local NetCDF — single file, glob pattern, or directory (auto-concatenated along time) Local / cloud Zarr — local .zarr stores or cloud URIs (s3://, gs://, az://) Copernicus Marine (CMEMS) — live remote download via copernicus:// URI CMIP6 (PANGEO / intake-esm) — via cmip6:// URI ISIMIP — via isimip:// URI ERA5 cloud — ARCO-ERA5 on Google Cloud (era5gcs://) or ERA5-PDS on AWS (era5aws://) Argo floats — Ifremer GDAC ERDDAP (argo://) ICES Oceanographic Database — via ices:// or ices-fp:// EMODnet Physics — via emodnet:// All sources support domain and time-range subsetting. Loaded data is returned as an xr.Dataset (or xr.DataArray when a single variable is requested).
CLI usage (data-loader) Local files # Show metadata only data-loader /data/model.nc --info # Load and print summary; save to NetCDF data-loader /data/model.nc --variable sst --output sst.nc # Domain and time subsetting (lon_min lon_max lat_min lat_max) data-loader /data/model.nc --variable sst \ --domain -5. 15. 50. 65. --time-range 2020-01-01 2020-12-31 \ --output sst_subset.nc Copernicus Marine (CMEMS) # Describe a dataset without downloading data-loader --cmems-describe cmems_obs-sst_atl_phy_nrt_l3s_P1D-m # Browse catalogue, optionally filter by keywords data-loader --cmems-catalog --output cmems_catalog.json data-loader --cmems-catalog --contains SST North Atlantic # Download with subsetting data-loader --copernicus cmems_obs-sst_atl_phy_nrt_l3s_P1D-m \ --variable sea_surface_temperature \ --domain -5. 15. 50. 65. \ --time-range 2023-01-01 2023-01-31 \ --output sst_jan2023.nc CMIP6 (PANGEO) # Browse / filter the catalog data-loader --cmip6-describe --source-id CESM2 --variable-id tos data-loader --cmip6-catalog --output pangeo_cmip6.json # Load a subset (SOURCE/EXPERIMENT/VARIABLE/TABLE/MEMBER[/GRID]) data-loader --cmip6 CESM2/historical/tos/Omon/r1i1p1f1 \ --domain -5. 15. 50. 65. --time-range 1990-01-01 1990-12-31 \ --output cesm2_tos.nc ERA5 from cloud (no CDS account required) # List available variable mappings data-loader --era5-varlist gcs data-loader --era5-varlist aws # Load from Google Cloud (ARCO-ERA5 Zarr, anonymous) data-loader --era5gcs rsds \ --domain -20. 30. 45. 75. --time-range 2000-01-01 2000-12-31 \ --output era5_rsds_2000.nc # Override automatic ERA5 variable-name mapping data-loader --era5gcs rsds --era5-name surface_solar_radiation_downwards \ --domain -20. 30. 45. 75. --time-range 2000-06-01 2000-06-30 \ --output era5_rsds_jun2000.nc # Override the ARCO store URL data-loader --era5gcs tas \ --era5-store gs://gcp-public-data-arco-era5/ar/1959-2022-full_37-1h-0p25deg-chunk-1.zarr-v2 \ --time-range 1990-01-01 1990-12-31 --output tas_1990.nc # Load from AWS S3 (ERA5-PDS, anonymous) data-loader --era5aws tas \ --domain -5. 15. 50. 65. --time-range 2010-01-01 2010-03-31 \ --output era5_tas_q1_2010.nc Argo floats # List parameter catalogue data-loader --argo-catalog # Load physical profiles for a domain data-loader --argo physical \ --domain -5. 15. 50. 65. --time-range 2022-01-01 2022-12-31 \ --output argo_ns_2022.nc # Load specific parameters, restrict to float WMO numbers data-loader --argo TEMP,PSAL --wmo 6903548 6903549 --output argo_floats.nc ICES Oceanographic Database data-loader --ices-catalog data-loader --ices physical \ --domain -5. 15. 50. 65. --time-range 2015-01-01 2020-12-31 \ --output ices_ns.nc YAML config data-loader --config examples/data_loader_examples.yaml --section sst_copernicus Common options Flag Meaning --domain LON_MIN LON_MAX LAT_MIN LAT_MAX Spatial bounding box; accepts −180..180 or 0..360 --time-range START END Date strings, e.g. 2020-01-01 2020-12-31 --variable / -v Single variable to extract --output / -o Save to .nc or .json --resample FREQ Downsample before saving: daily, monthly, 6h, etc. --info Print metadata only (local files) URI routing DataLoader.load_data() routes requests based on the URI scheme before any format auto-detection:
URI prefix / format Routed to cmip6://… cmip6_loader.CMIP6Loader copernicus://… copernicus_loader.CopernicusLoader isimip://… isimip_loader.ISIMIPLoader era5gcs://… era5_cloud_loader.ERA5CloudLoader (backend=gcs) era5aws://… era5_cloud_loader.ERA5CloudLoader (backend=aws) argo://… argo_loader.ArgoLoader ices://… ices_loader.ICESLoader ices-fp://… ices_loader.ICESLoader (fixed-platform) emodnet://… emodnet_loader.EMODnetLoader Local path ending .zarr or any :// path Zarr (xr.open_zarr) .csv / .tsv / .txt csv_loader.CSVLoader Everything else netcdf_loader.NetCDFLoader Loading local files NetCDF NetCDFLoader (in lib/netcdf_loader.py) handles single files, glob patterns, and directories. When a directory is given, all .nc files are found and concatenated along the time dimension (sorted by filename).
Triple-fallback time decoder — applied in order until one succeeds:
Standard xr.open_dataset with default time decoding cftime-based decoding (for proleptic_gregorian and other non-standard calendars) netCDF4.num2date direct conversion (for masked fill-value time axes and other broken encodings) Kelvin → Celsius auto-conversion — if a variable’s units attribute is "K" or "kelvin" and its values are plausibly in the Kelvin range (> 200 K), the loader subtracts 273.15 and updates the units to "°C" automatically.
Singleton squeezing — after loading, dimensions of size 1 that are not time/latitude/longitude are dropped from the returned DataArray so downstream code does not need to handle them.
Variable resolution When a specific variable is requested, DataLoader._extract_variable() tries:
Exact name match Only variable in dataset (single-variable file) Alias lookup via CoordinateMapper (reads config/name_mappings.yaml) The built-in fallback table maps common short names to known aliases:
Requested Also matches sst temp, temperature, tsurf, analysed_sst ssh zeta, eta, elevation, sea_surface_height sss salt, salinity, ssurf, analysed_sss temp temperature, potential_temperature, theta salt salinity, practical_salinity Directory loading When a directory path is given to load_model_data():
Looks for .nc files in the directory (then recursively if none found) If a variable is specified, prefers files whose stem contains the variable name Multiple matching files are concatenated along time and sorted Copernicus Marine Requires the copernicusmarine package:
pip install copernicusmarine Authentication uses the standard copernicusmarine credential store (run copernicusmarine login once to save credentials).
Key parameters passed to CopernicusLoader.load():
dataset_id — the CMEMS dataset identifier (e.g. cmems_obs-sst_atl_phy_nrt_l3s_P1D-m) variables — list of variable names to download domain — bounding box dict with lon_min, lon_max, lat_min, lat_max time_range — (start, end) strings forwarded directly to the CMEMS API minimum_depth / maximum_depth — optional depth range (metres) service — optional service override (e.g. arco-geo-series) The --cmems-describe flag calls CopernicusLoader.describe() and prints available datasets and services without downloading any data.
CMIP6 / PANGEO Requires intake, intake-esm, and gcsfs:
pip install intake intake-esm gcsfs Datasets are discovered via the PANGEO CMIP6 Zarr catalog at https://storage.googleapis.com/cmip6/pangeo-cmip6.json.
The URI format is:
cmip6://SOURCE_ID/EXPERIMENT_ID/VARIABLE_ID/TABLE_ID/MEMBER_ID[/GRID_LABEL] Example: cmip6://CESM2/historical/tos/Omon/r1i1p1f1
After loading, DataLoader automatically:
Sorts the time axis (intake-esm may concatenate chunks with inconsistent offsets) Normalises longitude from 0..360 to −180..180 if needed Results can be cached as compressed NetCDF by passing cache_dir to DataLoader:
loader = DataLoader(cache_dir='/data/cmip6_cache') ds = loader.load_data('cmip6://CESM2/historical/tos/Omon/r1i1p1f1', domain={'lon_min': -5, 'lon_max': 15, 'lat_min': 50, 'lat_max': 65}, time_range=('1990-01-01', '1990-12-31')) Zarr stores Dependencies pip install zarr # required for any Zarr pip install s3fs # S3 (AWS) pip install gcsfs # Google Cloud Storage pip install fsspec # HTTP/HTTPS Local Zarr Auto-detected when the path ends in .zarr:
ds = loader.load_data('/data/ocean/model.zarr', variable='sst') Cloud Zarr Cloud paths (s3://, gs://, az://) are also detected as Zarr automatically. Pass credentials via storage_options:
# Public S3 bucket ds = loader.load_data( 's3://public-ocean-data/era5.zarr', variable='sst', storage_options={'anon': True}, ) # GCS with service account ds = loader.load_data( 'gs://ocean-data-bucket/model.zarr', variable='sst', storage_options={'token': '/path/to/credentials.json'}, ) Chunking Pass a chunks dict or 'auto' to open the store with Dask lazy loading:
ds = loader.load_data( 's3://bucket/ocean.zarr', variable='sst', chunks={'time': 30, 'lat': 200, 'lon': 200}, storage_options={'anon': True}, ) Align requested chunks with the store’s native chunking to avoid read amplification. Use 'auto' to let xarray choose.
Consolidated metadata consolidated=True (the default) reads .zmetadata in a single request rather than fetching per-chunk metadata files. Disable only for stores that lack a consolidated metadata file:
ds = loader.load_data('model.zarr', consolidated=False) ERA5 on cloud (no CDS account) The era5gcs:// and era5aws:// URI schemes access public ERA5 cloud archives without a CDS account:
GCS: ARCO-ERA5 Zarr at gs://gcp-public-data-arco-era5/ (requires gcsfs) AWS: ERA5-PDS at s3://era5-pds/ (requires s3fs) Variable names follow CMIP6/CF conventions (tas, pr, rsds, …) and are mapped to ERA5 internal names automatically. Use --era5-varlist gcs or --era5-varlist aws to see the mapping table.
Programmatic use Basic import from lib.data_loader import DataLoader, load_model_data, load_observation_data # Convenience functions (create a fresh loader each call) ds = load_model_data('/data/model/', variable='ssh', time_range=('2020-01-01', '2020-12-31'), domain={'lon_min': -10, 'lon_max': 5, 'lat_min': 50, 'lat_max': 60}) # Reusable loader instance (shares CoordinateMapper, honours cache_dir) loader = DataLoader(cache_dir='/data/cache') ds = loader.load_data('copernicus://cmems_obs-sst_atl_phy_nrt_l3s_P1D-m', variable='sea_surface_temperature', domain={'lon_min': -5, 'lon_max': 15, 'lat_min': 50, 'lat_max': 65}, time_range=('2023-01-01', '2023-01-31')) Load from YAML config block DataLoader.load_from_config() reads an observations: block from a parsed YAML config and dispatches to the appropriate loader:
import yaml from lib.data_loader import DataLoader with open('my_config.yaml') as fh: cfg = yaml.safe_load(fh) ds = DataLoader.load_from_config(cfg, section='observations') Supported source: values in the config block: copernicus, cmip6, isimip, ices, era5gcs, era5aws, auto (local/cloud file, the default).
Example config block for Copernicus:
observations: source: copernicus dataset_id: cmems_obs-sst_atl_phy_nrt_l3s_P1D-m variables: [sea_surface_temperature] domain: lat_min: 50 lat_max: 65 lon_min: -15 lon_max: 15 time_range: [2020-01-01, 2020-12-31] File metadata from lib.data_loader import get_file_info info = get_file_info('/data/model.nc') # Returns dict with: path, variables, dimensions, coordinates, # time_steps, time_start, time_end, time_resolution, domain Caching Pass cache_dir to DataLoader to cache downloaded remote datasets as compressed NetCDF. On subsequent calls with the same parameters the local cache file is used instead of re-downloading:
loader = DataLoader(cache_dir='/data/cache') Cache files are named by an MD5 hash of the source identifier, variable, domain, and time range, so different parameter combinations never collide.
summary:
Data Loader Guide

Overview

data-loader (entry point for cli/data_loader.py) is the unified command for fetching and inspecting ocean data from multiple sources:

  • Local NetCDF — single file, glob pattern, or directory (auto-concatenated along time)
  • Local / cloud Zarr — local .zarr stores or cloud URIs (s3://, gs://, az://)
  • Copernicus Marine (CMEMS) — live remote download via copernicus:// URI
  • CMIP6 (PANGEO / intake-esm) — via cmip6:// URI
  • ISIMIP — via isimip:// URI
  • ERA5 cloud — ARCO-ERA5 on Google Cloud (era5gcs://) or ERA5-PDS on AWS (era5aws://)
  • Argo floats — Ifremer GDAC ERDDAP (argo://)
  • ICES Oceanographic Database — via ices:// or ices-fp://
  • EMODnet Physics — via emodnet://

All sources support domain and time-range subsetting. Loaded data is returned as an xr.Dataset (or xr.DataArray when a single variable is requested).
title: Data Loader

content:
Tidal Analysis Guide Overview tidal-analysis extracts tidal harmonic constituents from model output and validates them against satellite tidal atlases (FES2014, TPXO9) or tide gauge records from the GESLA database.
The harmonic analysis uses utide to fit sinusoidal components at each grid point or station location. Model amplitudes and phases are then compared to the reference atlas or gauge data.
Analysis modes Mode Flag Description spatial --mode spatial Harmonic analysis on the full model grid, compared to a tidal atlas gesla --mode gesla Harmonic analysis at GESLA tide gauge locations both --mode both Run spatial and GESLA together (default) stations --mode stations Named stations defined in config Config structure Annotated example: config/tidal_analysis.yaml
# config/tidal_analysis.yaml area: NS experiment: Baseline model: base_path: /data/model/NS/ filename_pattern: "ns_{year}.nc" years: [2015, 2022] # Constituents to analyse (subset of M2, S2, K1, O1, N2, M4, …) constituents: [M2, S2, K1, O1] analysis: mode: both # spatial | gesla | both | stations # Tidal atlas for spatial comparison atlas: type: fes2014 # fes2014 | tpxo9 path: /data/atlas/fes2014/ # GESLA tide gauge database for station comparison gesla: path: /data/gesla/GESLA3.0_ALL.nc min_years: 10 # minimum record length to include a gauge output: prefix: ns_tidal Required keys: area, experiment, model.base_path, model.filename_pattern.
Usage # Full run with default mode (both) tidal-analysis --config config/tidal_analysis.yaml # Spatial only tidal-analysis --config config/tidal_analysis.yaml --mode spatial # GESLA stations only tidal-analysis --config config/tidal_analysis.yaml --mode gesla # Single constituent tidal-analysis --config config/tidal_analysis.yaml --constituents M2 # Parallel processing (uses all CPUs by default) tidal-analysis --config config/tidal_analysis.yaml --workers 8 # Append to an existing statistics file (useful when running multiple datasets) tidal-analysis --config config/tidal_analysis.yaml --dataset-name "FES2014" \ --append-stats Output analyses/<AREA>/validations/<EXPERIMENT>/ ├── plots/ │ └── <domain>/ ← physics | bio │ └── <model>/ │ └── <period>/ ← e.g. 2015-2022 │ └── tidal/ │ ├── <prefix>_M2_amplitude.png — spatial map of M2 amplitude │ ├── <prefix>_M2_phase.png — spatial map of M2 phase │ ├── <prefix>_M2_amp_bias.png — model − atlas amplitude bias │ ├── <prefix>_gesla_M2.png — scatter: model vs gauge amplitude │ └── <prefix>_station_map.png — GESLA gauge locations └── tables/ └── <domain>/ └── <model>/ └── tidal/ ├── <prefix>_tidal_statistics.txt └── <prefix>_tidal_detailed_statistics.txt Statistics file format The statistics file contains one section per constituent with area-averaged metrics:
M2 Amplitude RMSE: 0.032 m Bias: -0.008 m Correlation: 0.987 M2 Phase RMSE: 4.2 deg Bias: 1.1 deg When --append-stats is used, each call adds a new section labelled with --dataset-name, making it easy to compare multiple atlases or gauge datasets in one file.
Performance The spatial mode runs utide.solve() at every model grid point. For large domains this is the bottleneck. Use --workers N to parallelise across grid rows (default: all available CPUs). A typical North Sea grid (200×300 points) with 8 years of hourly output takes 5–15 minutes depending on the number of constituents.
The GESLA mode only analyses at gauge locations (~a few hundred) and is much faster.

Gridded 2D Validation Guide Overview gridded-2d-validation compares any horizontal 2-D model field against a gridded observation product. Three layer modes are supported:
Layer Description Typical use surface 2-D field or top of 3-D output SST, SSS, SSH bottom Deepest wet cell of 3-D output Bottom temperature / salinity <depth_m> Horizontal slice at a fixed depth (m) Depth-layer climatology Multiple layers can be run in a single invocation.
Prerequisites Model NetCDF output accessible as a file-glob pattern A gridded observation file (local or Copernicus Marine / WOA) pip install -e . with the ocean-stats package Recommended observation sources Variable Source CMEMS product ID SST OSTIA (CMEMS) METOFFICE-GLO-SST-L4-REP-OBS-SST (analysed_sst) Bottom temperature NW Shelf reanalysis cmems_mod_nws_phy-bottomt_my_7km-2D_P1M-m Bottom salinity NW Shelf reanalysis cmems_mod_nws_phy-so_my_7km-3D_P1M-m Depth-level T/S WOA23 local file, NOAA/NCEI OSTIA SST is delivered in Kelvin — add offset: -273.15 in the obs config block.
Config file # config/gridded_2d_validation.yaml area: NS experiment: Baseline years: [2010, 2011, 2012] model: base_path: /data/model/NS/ filename_pattern: "getm_{year}*.nc" variable_map: temp: sst # rename model variable if needed variables: [temp] observations: temp: - name: OSTIA path: source: copernicus dataset_id: METOFFICE-GLO-SST-L4-REP-OBS-SST variable: analysed_sst offset: -273.15 processing: layers: [surface, bottom] # or a single string / list including depths chunking: time_chunk: 30 # days per processing chunk output: base_dir: ./analyses Config file on GitHub: config/gridded_2d_validation.yaml
Usage # Surface only (config default) gridded-2d-validation --config config/gridded_2d_validation.yaml # Surface + bottom in one run gridded-2d-validation --config config/gridded_2d_validation.yaml \ --layers surface bottom # Surface + 50 m depth slice + bottom gridded-2d-validation --config config/gridded_2d_validation.yaml \ --layers surface 50 bottom The --layers flag overrides processing.layers in the config.
Output Results are written to analyses/areas/<area>/validations/<experiment>/:
plots/<domain>/<model>/<period>/ surface/ ← time-mean maps, monthly bias maps, scatter plots bottom/ 50m/ tables/<domain>/<model>/ <variable>_validation_statistics.txt Statistics text files contain overall + monthly RMSE, bias, MAE, and correlation.
Notes Observations are regridded onto the model grid using bilinear interpolation (xESMF); weight files are cached by MD5 hash under regrid_weights/. For bottom layer: the deepest non-NaN cell is selected per column using sigma-coordinate-aware extraction. For depth slices: the model field is interpolated vertically to the requested depth before regridding.

Gridded 2D Validation Guide

Overview

gridded-2d-validation compares any horizontal 2-D model field against a gridded observation product. Three layer modes are supported:

Layer Description Typical use
surface 2-D field or top of 3-D output SST, SSS, SSH
bottom Deepest wet cell of 3-D output Bottom temperature / salinity
<depth_m> Horizontal slice at a fixed depth (m) Depth-layer climatology

Multiple layers can be run in a single invocation.

Prerequisites

  • Model NetCDF output accessible as a file-glob pattern
  • A gridded observation file (local or Copernicus Marine / WOA)
  • pip install -e . with the ocean-stats package

Recommended observation sources

Variable Source CMEMS product ID
SST OSTIA (CMEMS) METOFFICE-GLO-SST-L4-REP-OBS-SST (analysed_sst)
Bottom temperature NW Shelf reanalysis cmems_mod_nws_phy-bottomt_my_7km-2D_P1M-m
Bottom salinity NW Shelf reanalysis cmems_mod_nws_phy-so_my_7km-3D_P1M-m
Depth-level T/S WOA23 local file, NOAA/NCEI

OSTIA SST is delivered in Kelvin — add offset: -273.15 in the obs config block.

content:
Gridded 3D Validation Guide Overview gridded-3d-validation validates model temperature and salinity (or any 3-D variable) against a gridded observation product — WOA23 climatology, CMIP6 ensembles, Copernicus reanalysis, or any dataset that provides values at every horizontal grid point and a set of depth levels.
The observations are regridded onto the model grid and compared cell-by-cell, enabling:
Spatial statistics maps (RMSE, bias, MAE, correlation per grid point) Time-mean comparison maps at selected depth levels Monthly statistics bar charts Statistics text file (overall + monthly breakdown) When to use this tool vs profile validation:
Use gridded-3d-validation for climatologies or reanalyses that cover the full model domain at all depths. Use argo-profiles, cruise-ctd-profiles, or fixed-platform for sparse in-situ observations scattered in space and time.
Prerequisites Model NetCDF output accessible as a file-glob pattern A gridded 3-D observation product (WOA23, CMEMS reanalysis, CMIP6) pip install -e . with the ocean-stats package Recommended observation sources Variable Source Notes Temperature, salinity WOA23 (NOAA/NCEI) 12-month climatology, 1° Temperature, salinity GLORYS12 (CMEMS) cmems_mod_glo_phy_my_0.083deg_P1M-m Temperature, salinity CMIP6 ensemble Via PANGEO catalog (cmip6://) Config file # config/gridded_3d_validation.yaml area: NS experiment: Baseline years: [2010, 2011, 2012] model: base_path: /data/model/NS/ filename_pattern: "getm_{year}*.nc" variables: [temp, salt] observations: temp: - name: WOA23 path: /data/obs/woa23_decav_t00_04.nc variable: t_an salt: - name: WOA23 path: /data/obs/woa23_decav_s00_04.nc variable: s_an processing: depth_levels: [5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500] # depth slices to compare compute_spatial_stats: true chunking: time_chunk: 30 output: base_dir: ./analyses Config file on GitHub: config/gridded_3d_validation.yaml
Usage # Run with config file gridded-3d-validation --config config/gridded_3d_validation.yaml # Override depth levels on the command line gridded-3d-validation --config config/gridded_3d_validation.yaml \ --depths 5 50 100 200 Output Results are written to analyses/areas/<area>/validations/<experiment>/:
plots/<domain>/<model>/<period>/ 3d/ <variable>_bias_<depth>m.png <variable>_rmse_<depth>m.png <variable>_monthly_stats.png tables/<domain>/<model>/ <variable>_validation_statistics.txt Statistics text files contain overall RMSE, bias, MAE, and correlation plus a monthly breakdown.
Notes Observations are interpolated horizontally onto the model grid (xESMF bilinear); weight files are cached by MD5 hash under regrid_weights/. Observations are then interpolated vertically onto each depth_levels target using linear interpolation. If the obs product is a 12-month climatology (no explicit year axis), each model time step is matched to the corresponding calendar month.

content:
Profile Validation Guide Overview Five scripts share the same profile validation workflow:
Command Observation source argo-profiles Argo floats (Ifremer ERDDAP) glodap-profiles GLODAP v2.2023 bottle data wod-profiles World Ocean Database cruise-ctd-profiles ICES / EMODnet cruise CTD casts fixed-platform Moorings and fixed buoy profiles All five follow the same config structure, output layout, and command-line flags.
Config structure Annotated example configs: config/argo_profiles.yaml · config/glodap_profiles.yaml · config/cruise_ctd_profiles.yaml · config/wod_profiles.yaml · config/fixed_platform.yaml
# .stats.yaml (shared identity — set once per project) metadata: author: "K. Bolding" institute: "BB" project: "NorthSea-MFC" model: name: "pyGETM" output: base_dir: "./analyses" # config/argo_profiles.yaml (per-run) area: NS experiment: Baseline domain: lon_min: -5.0 lon_max: 10.0 lat_min: 50.0 lat_max: 62.0 years: [2015, 2022] # or year: 2020 for a single year variables: [TEMP, PSAL, DOXY] # subset of available variables model: base_path: /data/model/NS/ filename_pattern: "ns_{year}.nc" output: prefix: ns_argo # filename prefix for plots and tables Required keys: area, experiment, model.base_path, model.filename_pattern.
Common command-line flags All five scripts accept:
Flag Description --config FILE YAML config file --area NAME Override area --experiment NAME Override experiment --years START END Override year range --variables VAR … Override variables --analyses-dir DIR Override output.base_dir --no-staging Skip writing to simulation registry --save-residuals Write model-minus-obs residuals parquet (needed for mle-comparison) Script-specific flags:
argo-profiles: no extra flags glodap-profiles: --no-model (skip model comparison, obs diagnostics only) cruise-ctd-profiles: --dataset-name LABEL (tag the statistics section) fixed-platform: --station NAME (select a single named station) What each run produces analyses/<AREA>/validations/<EXPERIMENT>/ ├── plots/ │ └── <domain>/ ← physics | bio │ └── <model>/ ← e.g. pyGETM │ └── <period>/ ← e.g. 2020 | 2015-2022 │ └── argo/ ← or glodap/, wod/, cruise/, platform/ │ ├── <prefix>_overview.png │ ├── <prefix>_hovmoller_TEMP.png │ └── <prefix>_profiles_YYYY.png └── tables/ └── <domain>/ └── <model>/ └── argo/ └── <prefix>_profile_validation_statistics.txt The overview figure typically has 6 panels:
float/station/cruise map coloured by platform or time temperature vs depth scatter salinity vs depth scatter T-S diagram mean vertical profiles (normalised) profile or observation count per platform Running multiple experiments Run the script once per experiment, changing experiment: in the config (or passing --experiment). All outputs go into separate subdirectories:
argo-profiles --config config/argo_profiles.yaml --experiment Baseline argo-profiles --config config/argo_profiles.yaml --experiment ObsKd argo-profiles --config config/argo_profiles.yaml --experiment HighRes Generating residuals for MLE comparison Pass --save-residuals alongside the normal run. This writes an additional parquet file that mle-comparison consumes:
argo-profiles --config config/argo_profiles.yaml \ --experiment Baseline --save-residuals argo-profiles --config config/argo_profiles.yaml \ --experiment ObsKd --save-residuals Residuals are written to:
analyses/areas/<AREA>/validations/<EXPERIMENT>/argo_residuals.parquet The parquet has columns: time, lat, lon, depth, variable, value (obs), model_value, and optionally profile_id.
See guides/mle-comparison.md for the ranking workflow.
GLODAP-specific notes The GLODAP v2.2023 merged master file (~300 MB) is downloaded automatically on first run and cached locally. Set glodap.cache_dir in the config to control where it lands:
glodap: cache_dir: /data/obs/glodap/ qc_flags: [2] # 2 = good data; 0 includes unqualified Pass --no-model to produce observation-only diagnostics (station map, T-S diagram, profile climatology) without loading any model output.
Fixed-platform notes The fixed-platform script reads from the station catalogue (config/station_catalogue.yaml). Named stations include L4, E1, Stonehaven, Helgoland, and the MARNET and CEFAS SmartBuoy networks. Use download-station to fetch and cache station data before running fixed-platform.
download-station download --station L4 --output-dir /data/stations fixed-platform --config config/fixed_platform.yaml --station L4

Overview

Config structure

# .stats.yaml (shared identity — set once per project)

  author:    "K. Bolding"
  institute: "BB"
  project:   "NorthSea-MFC"
model:
  name:    "pyGETM"
output:
  base_dir: "./analyses"

# config/argo_profiles.yaml (per-run)
area:       NS
experiment: Baseline

domain:
  lon_min: -5.0
  lon_max: 10.0
  lat_min: 50.0
  lat_max: 62.0

years: [2015, 2022]           # or year: 2020 for a single year
variables: [TEMP, PSAL, DOXY] # subset of available variables

model:
  base_path:        /data/model/NS/

output:
  prefix: ns_argo             # filename prefix for plots and tables

Required keys: area, experiment, model.base_path, model.filename_pattern.
title: Profile Validation

content:
MLE Cross-Experiment Comparison Guide Overview Standard validation metrics (RMSE, bias, correlation) compare each experiment against observations in isolation. When you have three or more experiments that share the same observation dataset — sensitivity runs, ensemble members, or physics variants — a scalar metric gives no information about the joint structure of the errors.
MLE comparison in reduced-rank (SVD) space addresses this. The joint residual matrix across all experiments is decomposed with SVD, and each experiment is ranked by its Gaussian log-likelihood in the leading modes. This is the same “optimal fingerprinting” framework used in climate attribution (Allen & Tett 1999): the leading SVD modes capture the dominant joint variance pattern, and the log-likelihood in that low-dimensional space is a principled, multi-dimensional distance from perfect.
The result is a ranking table where the best experiment has Δlog L = 0 and all others have Δlog L < 0. A large negative Δlog L means that experiment’s residual pattern is substantially different from the best, in the directions that matter most across the ensemble.
When to use this You have three or more experiments with different physics, forcing, or grid settings. All experiments were evaluated against the same observation dataset (e.g. Argo floats or GLODAP profiles). The experiments cover overlapping time periods and geographic domains. It is less useful when:
You only have two experiments — the result collapses to a weighted RMSE comparison and provides no additional information. Experiments cover non-overlapping years — the common observation footprint will be empty or too small. How it works experiment DataFrames (time, lat, lon, depth, value, model_value, variable) │ ▼ 1. Common footprint Intersect observation keys present in ALL experiments with non-NaN obs and model values. │ ▼ 2. Residual matrix R [n_obs × n_exp] R[i, k] = model_value[i,k] − obs_value[i] │ ▼ 3. Standardise per variable Divide each variable's rows by the obs standard deviation so TEMP, PSAL, and DOXY are commensurable. │ ▼ 4. SVD → R_std ≈ U · diag(s) · Vt Select leading r modes by cumulative variance threshold (default 90%). │ ▼ 5. Project each experiment: r̃_k = U_r^T · R_std[:, k] shape (r,) │ ▼ 6. Log-likelihood: log L_k = −½ σ⁻² ‖r̃_k‖² Rank by Δlog L = log L_k − max(log L) Prerequisites Each experiment must first be run with --save-residuals to produce a residuals parquet file alongside the standard validation outputs:
argo-profiles --config config/argo_profiles.yaml --save-residuals The residuals file is written to:
analyses/areas/<area>/validations/<experiment>/argo_residuals.parquet Run once per experiment, changing the experiment: key in the config each time. All experiments must share the same area: and obs_type: settings so the observation footprint is comparable.
CLI usage Step 1: Generate residual files # Run for each experiment by editing experiment: in the config argo-profiles --config config/baseline.yaml --save-residuals argo-profiles --config config/sensitivity1.yaml --save-residuals argo-profiles --config config/sensitivity2.yaml --save-residuals Step 2: Run MLE comparison # Single obs source mle-comparison --analyses-dir ./analyses --area NS \ --experiments Baseline Sensitivity1 Sensitivity2 \ --obs-types argo --variables TEMP PSAL DOXY \ --diagnostics # Combine Argo (deep water) + fixed stations (coastal) for fuller coverage mle-comparison --analyses-dir ./analyses --area NS \ --experiments Baseline Sensitivity1 \ --obs-types argo fixed_platform --variables TEMP PSAL # Log-transform phytoplankton and oxygen before ranking mle-comparison --analyses-dir ./analyses --area NS \ --experiments Baseline Sensitivity1 \ --obs-types argo --variables TEMP PSAL CHLA DOXY \ --transforms CHLA:log DOXY:log Residuals from all --obs-types are concatenated per experiment before the common footprint is built, so each obs source contributes its locations.
Key options:
Flag Default Description --analyses-dir ./analyses Root of the analyses tree --area required Area code, e.g. NS --experiments required Two or more experiment names --obs-types required One or more: argo, glodap, wod, fixed_platform, … --variables all in data Variable names to include --transforms none Per-variable transform: VAR:TRANSFORM pairs. See below. --rank auto Force SVD rank --variance-threshold 0.90 Cumulative variance for auto rank --sigma2-method pooled One of pooled, median_exp, best_exp --diagnostics off Write scree plot, mode-loading plot, per-mode bar chart --output-dir auto Override output directory for results Variable transforms For biogeochemical variables whose distributions are right-skewed (phytoplankton chlorophyll, nutrients, turbidity), a Gaussian residual assumption is not appropriate in linear space. Applying a log-transform before the MLE makes the residuals approximately Gaussian:
Transform Applied as Use for log ln(model) − ln(obs) = ln(model/obs) Chlorophyll, POC, nutrients log10 log₁₀(model) − log₁₀(obs) Same; more interpretable magnitudes sqrt √model − √obs Weakly skewed count-like variables none / identity model − obs (default) Temperature, salinity, … Rows where the raw value or model value is ≤ 0 are dropped before the common footprint is built (a warning is logged for each experiment).
Using a config file # config/mle_comparison.yaml area: NS analyses_dir: ./analyses experiments: [Baseline, Sensitivity1, Sensitivity2] obs_types: [argo, fixed_platform] # list for multi-source, or a single string variables: [TEMP, PSAL, CHLA] transforms: CHLA: log # ln(model/obs) residuals for chlorophyll DOXY: log # ln(model/obs) residuals for dissolved oxygen rank: 3 diagnostics: true mle-comparison --config config/mle_comparison.yaml Command-line flags override config file values.
Output Results are written to analyses/areas/<area>/validations/mle_comparison/:
File Description mle_ranking.csv Machine-readable ranking table mle_ranking.txt Human-readable ranking summary scree_plot.png Singular values + cumulative explained variance mode_loadings_mode0.png Spatial scatter of top loadings for mode 0 (and mode 1, …) per_mode_contributions.png Grouped bar chart of per-mode log-likelihood contributions mle_ranking.csv columns:
Column Description experiment Experiment name log_likelihood Gaussian log-likelihood in SVD space rank 1 = best delta_log_L log_likelihood − max(log_likelihood); 0 for the best aic Akaike information criterion (−2 log L + 2) explained_variance_captured Fraction of joint variance in the chosen rank Interpreting results Only delta_log_L matters, not the absolute log-likelihood value. delta_log_L = 0 identifies the best experiment; more negative values indicate larger residuals in the joint SVD space.
With two experiments the result is equivalent to a weighted RMSE comparison. The method adds value from three experiments upward.
The scree plot shows how much variance each mode captures. If a single mode dominates, the ranking is essentially one-dimensional and driven by one error pattern. If several modes have similar singular values, the comparison is genuinely multi-dimensional.
The mode-loading plot shows which observation locations carry the most weight in a given SVD mode. Large loadings (large circles) at a depth/location mean that obs site is most informative for distinguishing the experiments. Use this to understand what physical processes or regions drive the ranking.
The per-mode contributions bar chart shows how each experiment’s log-likelihood is distributed across modes. An experiment that is good in mode 0 but poor in mode 1 will show up here; if mode 1 is small relative to mode 0, the overall ranking is unaffected.
AIC (−2 log L + 2) can be used in a model-selection sense: prefer the experiment with the lowest AIC. The + 2 term penalises the single free parameter σ². For the purposes of cross-experiment ranking, AIC and Δlog L give the same ordering.
Rank selection The default automatic selection combines two criteria:
Variance threshold: smallest r where cumulative explained variance ≥ variance_threshold (default 0.90). Elbow: the r at the largest second-difference drop in the singular value spectrum. The chosen rank is min(threshold_r, elbow_r), clamped to [1, n_modes − 1].
Override with --rank when you have domain knowledge about how many independent error patterns to expect. Too low a rank discards real signal; too high a rank projects onto noise and compresses the ranking. The scree plot is the diagnostic to consult.
Programmatic use from lib.mle_comparison import compare_experiments_mle # experiment_dfs: dict of experiment_name -> DataFrame result = compare_experiments_mle( experiment_dfs, variables=['TEMP', 'PSAL', 'DOXY'], variance_threshold=0.90, return_diagnostics=True, ) ranking_df, diag = result print(ranking_df) # Diagnostics dict keys: # singular_values, explained_variance, chosen_rank, # U_r, Vt_r, r_tilde, sigma2, meta_df, scalings Each DataFrame in experiment_dfs must have columns: time, lat, lon, depth, value, model_value, variable and optionally profile_id.
Scope: profile observations only mle-comparison is designed for sparse profile observations (Argo, GLODAP, WOD, cruise CTD). It should not be used with gridded 2D or 3D model output, and here is why.
The log-likelihood formula assumes independent observations:
log L_k = −½ σ⁻² ‖r̃_k‖² For gridded data this assumption is badly violated. A 0.1° North Sea grid might have 50,000 points but a spatial correlation length of ~50 km, giving perhaps a few hundred effective degrees of freedom. The σ² estimate absorbs the nominal n_obs rather than the effective one, so it is wrong by a large factor. Δlog L values will look large and significant even when the true difference between experiments is small, and a genuine but tiny improvement can be made to appear decisive simply by using a finer grid.
For 3D gridded output the problem is compounded: neighbouring depth levels are also highly correlated, further inflating the apparent signal.
The SVD step itself is sound for gridded data — the leading modes behave like EOFs and capture the dominant spatial patterns of disagreement — but the likelihood ranking built on top of it is not meaningful without properly accounting for spatial covariance, which is intractable at grid scale.
For gridded validation experiments the scalar metrics, Taylor diagrams, and regional skill scores already in the codebase are the appropriate tool. If a pattern-based multi-experiment comparison is needed for gridded data, a useful intermediate approach is to spatially average to regional boxes first and then apply MLE to the box means, where the independence assumption is more defensible.
Computational cost The method is not computationally intensive. The matrix being decomposed is n_obs × n_exp, and n_exp is always small (typically 3–10).
Step Complexity Typical size Cost Build common index / key intersection O(n_obs × n_exp) 100k obs, 5 exp negligible DataFrame alignment and sorting O(n_obs log n_obs) — fast SVD of R_std O(n_obs × n_exp²) 100k × 5 seconds Project, log-L, ranking O(r × n_exp) 3 × 5 negligible The SVD is the mathematically dominant step, but because n_exp is tiny scipy uses the thin SVD and the cost scales linearly with n_obs. A 100,000 × 5 matrix takes well under a second.
The practical bottleneck is reading and joining the parquet files, not the maths. The residual matrix R lives entirely in RAM: 1 million obs × 10 experiments × 8 bytes = 80 MB, which is not a concern for any realistic profile dataset.
If pointed at gridded data — which the Scope section above discourages — n_obs could reach tens of millions and both the SVD and in-memory matrix would become genuinely expensive. For profile observations it will always feel instant.
Limitations Common obs footprint required: all experiments must cover the same stations and years. Experiments with non-overlapping periods cannot be compared. Diagonal covariance: observation errors are treated as independent and equal within each variable. Spatial correlation between obs is ignored. This is a reasonable approximation for scattered profile data but breaks down for gridded output (see Scope above). Low power with few experiments: with fewer than four experiments the SVD modes are poorly constrained and rankings may be unreliable. Pooled σ²: the noise variance is estimated across all experiments. If one experiment is catastrophically wrong, it inflates σ² and compresses the Δlog L differences among the others.
summary:
MLE Cross-Experiment Comparison Guide

Overview

Standard validation metrics (RMSE, bias, correlation) compare each experiment against observations in isolation. When you have three or more experiments that share the same observation dataset — sensitivity runs, ensemble members, or physics variants — a scalar metric gives no information about the joint structure of the errors.

MLE comparison in reduced-rank (SVD) space addresses this. The joint residual matrix across all experiments is decomposed with SVD, and each experiment is ranked by its Gaussian log-likelihood in the leading modes. This is the same “optimal fingerprinting” framework used in climate attribution (Allen & Tett 1999): the leading SVD modes capture the dominant joint variance pattern, and the log-likelihood in that low-dimensional space is a principled, multi-dimensional distance from perfect.
title: MLE Comparison

content:
run-validation Guide Overview run-validation is the master orchestrator. It reads a single master_config.yaml and runs all configured analysis steps in sequence, handling logging, error recovery, and simulation registry updates.
Use it when you want to run all validation types for an experiment in one command. For individual analysis types, run the dedicated scripts directly (e.g. argo-profiles, tidal-analysis).
Config structure Individual per-analysis configs are in config/: config/gridded_2d_validation.yaml · config/tidal_analysis.yaml · config/argo_profiles.yaml
# master_config.yaml experiment: area: NS experiment_id: Baseline name: "Baseline run 2015–2022" model: base_path: /data/model/NS/ filename_pattern: "ns_{year}.nc" name: pyGETM version: "" output: base_dir: ./analyses workflow: years: [2015, 2022] analyses: gridded_2d: enabled: true config: config/gridded_2d_validation.yaml gridded_3d: enabled: false config: config/gridded_3d_validation.yaml tidal_analysis: enabled: true config: config/tidal_analysis.yaml argo_profiles: enabled: true config: config/argo_profiles.yaml cruise_ctd_profiles: enabled: false config: config/cruise_ctd_profiles.yaml Required top-level sections: experiment, analyses, output, workflow.
Usage # Run all enabled steps run-validation --config master_config.yaml # Run a single step (useful for debugging or re-running one analysis) run-validation --config master_config.yaml --step tidal_analysis # List all recognised step names run-validation --list-steps Step names Step name Analysis run gridded_2d gridded-2d-validation gridded_3d gridded-3d-validation tidal_analysis tidal-analysis argo_profiles argo-profiles glodap_profiles glodap-profiles wod_profiles wod-profiles cruise_ctd_profiles cruise-ctd-profiles fixed_platform fixed-platform Error handling Each step runs independently. If one step fails it is logged and the orchestrator continues with the remaining steps. Failed steps are listed in the final summary. Exit code is non-zero if any step failed.
Partial runs with –step --step is useful when:
A single step failed and needs to be re-run after fixing the config. You want to add a new analysis type without re-running everything. You are debugging a specific script. # Re-run only tidal analysis after fixing the tidal config run-validation --config master_config.yaml --step tidal_analysis Relationship to individual scripts run-validation delegates to the same individual scripts that you can call directly. The per-step config files (e.g. config/argo_profiles.yaml) are passed through unchanged. The master config adds the experiment block that the individual scripts normally get from .stats.yaml or command-line flags.
For fine-grained control — custom flags, --no-model, --save-residuals — run the individual scripts directly instead of going through the orchestrator.

Scenario Analysis Guide Overview The scenario workflow has three stages:
CMIP6 forcing ──► bc correct ──► bias-corrected forcing │ ocean model (future run) │ ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐ single-scenario multi-scenario (trends, changes) (SSP comparison) Bias-correct CMIP6 atmospheric forcing against ERA5 (or equivalent) so its statistical properties match the observational reference over the calibration period. Run the ocean model with the corrected forcing for each SSP scenario. Analyse the ocean output — trend maps, time series, regional means — for one scenario at a time or across multiple scenarios simultaneously. Step 1 — Bias correction Commands Command Role bc correct Apply bias correction → write corrected NetCDF bc diagnostics Inspect correction → maps, seasonal cycles, trend plots Aliases: bc-correct, bc-diagnostics.
Specialised variable guides Some variables require extra treatment beyond a straightforward QDM:
Guide Variable What it covers Humidity bias correction huss Convert ERA5 d2m + sp → specific humidity before QDM Radiation bias correction net_sw, net_lw Correct net (not downwelling-only) radiation using ERA5 ssr/str and CMIP6 rsds−rsus/rlds−rlus; confirms CMIP6 availability River projection river flow & loads Delta-change method for freshwater inputs using bias-corrected P/E Correction methods Method Flag Description Quantile delta mapping qdm Preserves relative future trends; corrects full distribution Quantile mapping qm Corrects distribution but suppresses climate change signal Delta (additive) delta Fast; corrects mean only qdm is the recommended default for most variables.
Config structure # config/bc.yaml metadata: author: "K. Bolding" institute: "BB" project: "NorthSea-MFC" area: NS experiment: ssp245 scenario: ssp245 domain: lon_min: -5.0; lon_max: 10.0 lat_min: 50.0; lat_max: 62.0 calibration: start: "1990-01-01" end: "2014-12-31" future: start: "2015-01-01" end: "2100-12-31" correction: method: qdm n_quantiles: 100 variables: - name: tas kind: additive # additive or multiplicative - name: pr kind: multiplicative reference: # ERA5 or equivalent observational reference tas: source: local path: /data/era5/tas_{year}.nc variable: t2m pr: source: local path: /data/era5/pr_{year}.nc variable: tp historical: # CMIP6 historical member tas: uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/historical/tas/day/r1i1p1f1" pr: uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/historical/pr/day/r1i1p1f1" future_model: # CMIP6 future scenario member tas: uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/ssp245/tas/day/r1i1p1f1" pr: uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/ssp245/pr/day/r1i1p1f1" output: dir: ./bc_output/{scenario} filename_pattern: "{variable}_bc_{year}.nc" compress: true Config on GitHub: config/bias_correction_example.yaml
Running the correction # Full run bc correct --config config/bc.yaml # Single variable / override method / override scenario bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --variable tas bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --method qdm bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --scenario ssp585 # Stages: correction only or disaggregation only bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --steps correct bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --steps disaggregate Disaggregation distributes daily corrected output to sub-daily resolution using a diurnal ERA5 climatology. Requires a disaggregation: block in the config.
Correction output bc_output/ssp245/ tas_bc_2015.nc … tas_bc_2100.nc pr_bc_2015.nc … pr_bc_2100.nc Running diagnostics bc diagnostics --config config/bc.yaml bc diagnostics --config config/bc.yaml --variable tas bc diagnostics --config config/bc.yaml --output-dir ./diag/ssp245/ Results land under analyses/areas/<area>/scenarios/<scenario>/:
analyses/areas/NS/scenarios/ssp245/ └── tas/ ├── plots/ │ ├── calibration_maps.png — reference | CMIP6 raw | bias │ ├── seasonal_cycle.png — monthly climatology comparison │ ├── future_trend.png — annual means + linear trend to 2100 │ └── monthly_breakdown.png — decadal heatmap └── tables/ └── statistics.txt — per-month RMSE / bias / correlation Multiple scenarios for scenario in ssp245 ssp370 ssp585; do bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --scenario $scenario bc diagnostics --config config/bc.yaml --scenario $scenario done Step 2 — Single scenario analysis single-scenario analyses ocean model output from one future projection: trends, long-term changes, regional means, and extreme-value analysis.
Config # config/single_scenario.yaml area: NS experiment: ssp245 model: base_path: /data/model/NS/{experiment}/ filename_pattern: "getm_{year}*.nc" variables: [temp, salt] scenario: experiment: ssp245 reference_period: start: "1985-01-01" end: "2014-12-31" future_period: start: "2070-01-01" end: "2099-12-31" output: base_dir: ./analyses Config on GitHub: config/single_scenario.yaml
Usage single-scenario --config config/single_scenario.yaml single-scenario --config config/single_scenario.yaml --scenario ssp585 Output analyses/areas/NS/validations/ssp245/ plots/ temp_trend_map.png — linear trend per grid point temp_timeseries.png — area-mean annual time series temp_change_map.png — future − reference period mean tables/ trend_statistics.txt Step 3 — Multi-scenario comparison multi-scenario loads output from multiple SSP runs and overlays them — time series, spatial change maps, spread envelopes.
Config # config/multi_scenario.yaml area: NS model: base_path: /data/model/NS/{experiment}/ filename_pattern: "getm_{year}*.nc" variables: [temp, salt] scenarios: - name: SSP1-2.6 experiment: ssp126 - name: SSP2-4.5 experiment: ssp245 - name: SSP5-8.5 experiment: ssp585 reference_period: start: "1985-01-01" end: "2014-12-31" output: base_dir: ./analyses Config on GitHub: config/multi_scenario.yaml
Usage multi-scenario --config config/multi_scenario.yaml Output analyses/areas/NS/multi_scenario/ plots/ temp_timeseries_comparison.png — all SSPs on one axis temp_change_map_ssp585.png — spatial change per scenario temp_spread_envelope.png — min/max across scenarios tables/ multi_scenario_summary.txt Performance notes Bias correction uses Dask lazy loading; memory stays bounded because output is written year by year. Diagnostics use a time-only chunk path — typically ~2 s/year. Regridding weights are cached under cache.dir and reused across variables and scenarios with the same grids.

content:
Humidity Bias Correction Guide Background: two ways to express atmospheric humidity The atmosphere can hold a limited amount of water vapour, and that limit rises strongly with temperature. Near-surface humidity is routinely reported in two different forms:
Dew-point temperature (d2m, unit K or °C) is the temperature to which a parcel of air must be cooled at constant pressure before water vapour begins to condense. At the dew point the air is exactly saturated — its actual vapour pressure equals the saturation vapour pressure. ERA5 stores this as the 2 m dew-point temperature.
Specific humidity (huss, unit kg kg⁻¹) is the mass of water vapour per unit mass of moist air. It is the quantity most directly useful for computing latent heat fluxes and for comparing model thermodynamics. CMIP6 models output this variable directly.
Dataset Variable Units Description ERA5 d2m K 2 m dew-point temperature ERA5 sp Pa Surface pressure (companion variable) CMIP6 huss kg kg⁻¹ Near-surface specific humidity Because bias correction requires a common currency, ERA5 d2m must be converted to specific humidity before the correction is applied.
Step 1 — Convert ERA5 d2m to specific humidity 1a. Saturation vapour pressure from dew-point temperature (Buck equation) At the dew-point temperature the air is exactly saturated, so the actual vapour pressure equals the saturation vapour pressure at that temperature. The Buck (1981) equation gives the saturation vapour pressure over a liquid water surface as a function of temperature:
e(Td) = 6.1121 × exp( 17.368 × Td_C / (238.88 + Td_C) ) [hPa] where Td_C = d2m [K] − 273.15 is the dew-point in °C.
Constants explained:
Constant Value Meaning 6.1121 hPa Saturation vapour pressure of water at 0 °C — the reference point of the Clausius–Clapeyron curve. The formula returns this value when Td_C = 0. 17.368 dimensionless Empirical scaling of the Clausius–Clapeyron exponent, fitted to laboratory measurements of saturated water vapour over a liquid surface in the 0–50 °C range. 238.88 °C Empirical offset in the denominator; together with 17.368 it shapes how steeply vapour pressure rises with temperature. Physically it absorbs the effective activation energy of evaporation from the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. The exponential form is a simplified solution to the Clausius–Clapeyron differential equation, which states that the slope of the saturation vapour pressure curve is proportional to e / T² (latent heat and gas-law terms absorbed into the empirical constants). Buck’s coefficients are fitted to measured data and are more accurate than earlier Magnus-formula variants, particularly in the 0–50 °C range relevant for NE-Atlantic forcing.
1b. Specific humidity from vapour pressure Once the actual vapour pressure e is known, specific humidity follows from the ideal gas law. Water vapour (molar mass M_w = 18.015 g mol⁻¹) and dry air (molar mass M_d = 28.964 g mol⁻¹) obey Dalton’s law of partial pressures:
q = ε × e / (p_hPa − (1 − ε) × e) Constants explained:
Symbol Value Meaning ε 0.622 Ratio of molar masses M_w / M_d = 18.015 / 28.964. Not an empirical fit — it is an exact physical constant. Water vapour is lighter than dry air, so humid air is less dense than dry air at the same pressure and temperature. p_hPa Pa / 100 Total surface pressure in hPa (ERA5 sp divided by 100). e hPa Actual vapour pressure from step 1a. The denominator p_hPa − (1 − ε) × e is the effective partial pressure of the dry-air component. At typical atmospheric conditions (e ≈ 10–30 hPa, p ≈ 1000 hPa) the correction term (1 − ε) × e is small (< 1 %), so the simpler approximation q ≈ ε × e / p introduces negligible error. The full form is used here for correctness.
The result q is in kg kg⁻¹ — directly comparable to CMIP6 huss.
Note on surface pressure: ERA5 sp is required as a companion variable. Set sp_variable: sp and sp_path pointing to the ERA5 surface-pressure file. Using the standard atmosphere value p₀ = 1013.25 hPa in place of actual sp introduces < 1 % error in q for mid-latitude conditions — acceptable if sp is unavailable, but the full pressure field is preferred for coastal and orographically complex regions.
Step 2 — Bias correction (QDM, multiplicative) Specific humidity is a non-negative, bounded ratio. It therefore uses multiplicative Quantile Delta Mapping (QDM) — the same treatment applied to precipitation (pr).
Input: ref = q derived from ERA5 d2m + sp (calibration period, e.g. 1990–2019) hist = CMIP6 historical huss (calibration period) future = CMIP6 future huss (projection period) Output: huss_corrected (projection period, kg kg⁻¹) Multiplicative QDM works in two steps per future time step:
Compute the ratio future / hist_quantile_match — the model-projected relative change in humidity, which is preserved. Apply that ratio to the corresponding ERA5 reference quantile — the corrected value inherits the ERA5 climatological distribution while retaining the CMIP6 trend signal. This is preferable to additive correction for humidity because relative changes (e.g. +5 % above climatology) are physically more meaningful than additive offsets, and the multiplicative form cannot produce negative values.
Step 3 — Optional: back-convert to dew-point temperature If the ocean model requires d2m rather than huss, invert the conversion:
e = q × p_hPa / (ε + (1 − ε) × q) # vapour pressure [hPa] Td_C = 238.88 × ln(e / 6.1121) / (17.368 − ln(e / 6.1121)) d2m [K] = Td_C + 273.15 This is the algebraic inverse of steps 1a and 1b.
Configuration Add huss to config/bc.yaml (see also config/bias_correction_example.yaml):
variables: - name: huss kind: "*" # multiplicative — specific humidity is non-negative regridding: method: bilinear # intensive quantity (ratio), not a flux reference: huss: source: local path: "/data/ERA5/era5_d2m_{year}.nc" variable: d2m # triggers d2m → q conversion automatically sp_variable: sp sp_path: "/data/ERA5/era5_sp_{year}.nc" historical: huss: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/historical/huss/day/r1i1p1f1" future_model: huss: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/{scenario}/huss/day/r1i1p1f1" The sp_variable key is the trigger: when present, bias_correct_forcings.py calls d2m_to_huss(d2m, sp) before passing the result to the QDM pipeline. No extra flags are needed.
Regridding note: bilinear is correct for huss. Conservative interpolation — which preserves the area-integrated total — is reserved for extensive flux variables (pr, rsds, rlds). Specific humidity is an intensive state variable like tas, uas, vas, and psl, so bilinear interpolation of the point value is appropriate.
Temporal disaggregation If sub-daily forcing is required, huss should be disaggregated for consistency with the other continuously-varying variables (tas, wind, psl). The diurnal amplitude of specific humidity is modest (~5–15 % of the daily mean) but it feeds directly into the latent heat flux, so omitting it while disaggregating tas would introduce an inconsistency.
Add a huss block to the disaggregation.variables section:
disaggregation: variables: huss: era5_path: "/data/ERA5/era5_d2m_{year}.nc" era5_variable: d2m # triggers d2m → q conversion before diurnal ratio sp_variable: sp sp_path: "/data/ERA5/era5_sp_{year}.nc" The sp_variable key triggers the same d2m_to_huss() conversion used in the reference-loading stage. The diurnal ratio is then built from sub-daily q = f(d2m, sp) and applied multiplicatively to the daily bias-corrected huss:
huss_hourly(t, h) = huss_daily_bc(t) × ERA5_q_diurnal_ratio(DOY(t), h) where ERA5_q_diurnal_ratio = ERA5_q_hour / ERA5_q_daily_mean.
No scale_factor is needed: the ratio computation cancels any unit offset between the raw d2m input and the derived q.
Running # Include huss in a full bias correction run (correction + disaggregation) bc correct --config config/bc.yaml # Correct only huss (skip disaggregation) bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --variable huss --steps correct # Disaggregate only (uses previously written BC files) bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --variable huss --steps disaggregate # Run diagnostics for huss bc diagnostics --config config/bc.yaml --variable huss Key references Buck (1981), New equations for computing vapor pressure and enhancement factor, J. Appl. Meteor. 20, 1527–1532. — Source of the 6.1121 / 17.368 / 238.88 constants; optimised for the 0–50 °C range over liquid water. Alduchov & Eskridge (1996), Improved Magnus Form Approximation of Saturation Vapor Pressure, J. Appl. Meteor. 35, 601–609. — Alternative coefficients for the same functional form. Cannon et al. (2015), Bias Correction of GCM Precipitation by Quantile Mapping, J. Climate 28, 6938–6959. — Derivation and properties of the QDM algorithm.

Humidity Bias Correction Guide

Background: two ways to express atmospheric humidity

The atmosphere can hold a limited amount of water vapour, and that limit rises strongly with temperature. Near-surface humidity is routinely reported in two different forms:

Dew-point temperature (d2m, unit K or °C) is the temperature to which a parcel of air must be cooled at constant pressure before water vapour begins to condense. At the dew point the air is exactly saturated — its actual vapour pressure equals the saturation vapour pressure. ERA5 stores this as the 2 m dew-point temperature.
title: Humidity Bias Correction

content:
Radiation Bias Correction Guide Background: downwelling vs net radiation Surface radiation comes in two forms relevant for bias correction:
Downwelling radiation is what the atmosphere delivers to the surface — shortwave (ssrd / rsds) from the sun and longwave (strd / rlds) from greenhouse gases and clouds.
Net radiation is what the surface actually exchanges after reflection and re-emission. For shortwave this is ssr = ssrd − reflected SW; for longwave str = strd − surface upwelling LW. Net radiation is the physically relevant quantity for the ocean heat balance.
Quantity ERA5 CMIP6 Formula Downwelling SW ssrd (J m⁻²) rsds (W m⁻²) — Downwelling LW strd (J m⁻²) rlds (W m⁻²) — Upwelling SW — rsus (W m⁻²) surface reflected Upwelling LW — rlus (W m⁻²) Stefan–Boltzmann emission Net SW ssr (J m⁻²) rsds − rsus (W m⁻²) always ≥ 0 Net LW str (J m⁻²) rlds − rlus (W m⁻²) always ≤ 0 ERA5 and CMIP6 models use different surface albedo and emissivity parameterisations. Correcting only the downwelling component (old approach: ssrd → rsds, strd → rlds) leaves the albedo-driven bias in the upwelling component uncorrected. Correcting the net removes this bias because the ERA5 net already reflects ERA5’s observed-constrained surface properties.
pyGETM accepts net radiation directly (alongside the downwelling-only and calculated-flux modes), making this the cleanest choice for future projections.
CMIP6 variable availability Both rsus and rlus are widely published at daily frequency in CMIP6 ScenarioMIP. ESGF searches (April 2026) confirm:
Variable Experiment Models with daily data MPI-ESM1-2-HR rsus historical 20+ ✓ 21 datasets, 1850–2014 rsus ssp585 33 models, 567 datasets ✓ 3 ensemble members rsus ssp245 31 models ✓ available rlus historical 20+ ✓ 21 datasets, 1850–2014 rlus ssp585 38 models, 618 datasets ✓ 3 ensemble members rlus ssp245 37 models, 857 datasets ✓ 5 ensemble members Notable exceptions and caveats:
GFDL-ESM4 publishes rsus/rlus at 3-hourly frequency only (not daily). Add resample: daily inside each composite component config block. The _load_composite function applies resampling component-wise before subtracting. ESGF queries performed on the ORNL node (via LLNL redirect): rsus ssp585 · rlus ssp585 · CMIP6 rsus publication list · CMIP6 rlus publication list Sign conventions and correction kind Variable Typical range kind Reason net_sw = rsds − rsus ≥ 0 W m⁻² * multiplicative Ratio of like-signed positives; preserves the QDM trend as a fractional change net_lw = rlds − rlus ≤ 0 W m⁻² + additive Signed quantity; additive delta is physically more meaningful than a ratio of negatives ERA5 str is negative (same sign as rlds − rlus) — no sign flip is needed. ERA5 ssr is positive — no sign flip needed.
Both ERA5 variables are stored as hourly J m⁻² accumulations. Convert to W m⁻² with scale_factor: 0.000277778 (= 1 / 3600) and resample: daily before the QDM correction.
Method: Quantile Delta Mapping on net fluxes ERA5 ssr (calibration period, daily W m⁻²) → ref CMIP6 rsds − rsus (historical, calibration period) → hist CMIP6 rsds − rsus (future, SSP period) → future QDM (kind='*'): corrected_net_sw = ref_quantile × (future / hist_quantile) The same structure applies to net_lw with kind='+':
corrected_net_lw = ref_quantile + (future − hist_quantile) QDM preserves the CMIP6 model’s projected trend at each quantile while anchoring the distribution to the ERA5-observed climatology.
Output The corrected fields are written as daily W m⁻² NetCDF files with CF metadata:
Variable long_name standard_name units net_sw Surface Net Shortwave Radiation surface_net_downward_shortwave_flux W m⁻² net_lw Surface Net Longwave Radiation surface_net_downward_longwave_flux W m⁻² pyGETM reads these directly. No decomposition back to downwelling + upwelling components is needed.
Temporal disaggregation ERA5 ssr and str provide the sub-daily diurnal cycle of net radiation directly. The diurnal cycle of ssr shows a clear daytime peak and nighttime zero, consistent with the multiplicative (*) kind. The diurnal cycle of str has a small amplitude (dominated by the near-constant strd) consistent with the additive (+) kind.
Add net_sw and net_lw blocks to disaggregation.variables:
disaggregation: variables: net_sw: era5_path: "/data/ERA5/kaj/era5_ssr_{year}.nc" era5_variable: ssr scale_factor: 0.000277778 # J/m² → W/m² net_lw: era5_path: "/data/ERA5/kaj/era5_str_{year}.nc" era5_variable: str scale_factor: 0.000277778 The disaggregated output is at ERA5 sub-daily frequency (hourly or 3-hourly) in W m⁻².
Configuration Full annotated configuration is in config/bias_correction_example.yaml. Key sections:
# ── Variables ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── variables: - name: net_sw kind: "*" regridding: method: conservative - name: net_lw kind: "+" regridding: method: conservative # ── Observational reference (ERA5 net radiation, single variable) ───────────── reference: net_sw: source: local path: "/data/ERA5/kaj/era5_ssr_{year}.nc" variable: ssr scale_factor: 0.000277778 resample: daily net_lw: source: local path: "/data/ERA5/kaj/era5_str_{year}.nc" variable: str scale_factor: 0.000277778 resample: daily # ── Historical CMIP6 (composite: rsds − rsus, rlds − rlus) ──────────────────── historical: net_sw: composite: minuend: # rsds — downwelling shortwave source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/historical/rsds/day/r1i1p1f1" subtrahend: # rsus — upwelling shortwave source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/historical/rsus/day/r1i1p1f1" net_lw: composite: minuend: # rlds — downwelling longwave source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/historical/rlds/day/r1i1p1f1" subtrahend: # rlus — upwelling longwave source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/historical/rlus/day/r1i1p1f1" # ── Future CMIP6 (same composite pattern) ───────────────────────────────────── future_model: net_sw: composite: minuend: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/{scenario}/rsds/day/r1i1p1f1" subtrahend: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/{scenario}/rsus/day/r1i1p1f1" net_lw: composite: minuend: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/{scenario}/rlds/day/r1i1p1f1" subtrahend: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://MPI-ESM1-2-HR/{scenario}/rlus/day/r1i1p1f1" GFDL-ESM4 (3-hourly rsus/rlus) GFDL-ESM4 does not publish rsus/rlus at daily frequency; only 3-hourly and monthly are available. The local files at /data/CMIP6/ssp585-GFDL-ESM4/rsus.nc contain 3-hourly data (shape (251120, 37, 38) = 86 years × 365 × 8 steps/day). Add resample: daily to each component block:
net_sw: composite: minuend: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://GFDL-ESM4/{scenario}/rsds/3hr/r1i1p1f1" resample: daily subtrahend: source: cmip6 uri: "cmip6://GFDL-ESM4/{scenario}/rsus/3hr/r1i1p1f1" resample: daily CMIP7 note: direct net radiation CMIP7 may publish surface net radiation directly (candidate names: rsns for net shortwave, rlns for net longwave). If so, replace the composite blocks in historical and future_model with simple single-source configs:
historical: net_sw: source: cmip7 # hypothetical source type uri: "cmip7://Model/historical/rsns/day/r1i1p1f1" net_lw: source: cmip7 uri: "cmip7://Model/historical/rlns/day/r1i1p1f1" The reference entries (ERA5 ssr/str) and the disaggregation entries remain unchanged — ERA5 already provides the net directly. The variables, kind, and correction sections need no changes. This is the main benefit of the net-radiation approach: the config structure is identical regardless of whether the CMIP model publishes the net directly or requires a composite.
Running # Correct net_sw and net_lw (all steps: correction + disaggregation) bc correct --config config/bc.yaml # Correct only the radiation variables bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --variable net_sw net_lw # Correction only (skip disaggregation) bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --variable net_sw net_lw --steps correct # Disaggregate from previously written BC files bc correct --config config/bc.yaml --variable net_sw net_lw --steps disaggregate # Diagnostics bc diagnostics --config config/bc.yaml --variable net_sw net_lw Key references Trenberth, K.E. & Fasullo, J.T. (2012), Tracking Earth’s energy: From El Niño to global warming, Surv. Geophys. 33, 413–426. — Global radiation budget components and their observational constraints. Cannon, A.J., Sobie, S.R. & Murdock, T.Q. (2015), Bias correction of GCM precipitation by quantile mapping: How well do methods preserve changes in quantiles and extremes?, J. Climate 28, 6938–6959. — QDM derivation. Evaluation of Surface Upward Longwave Radiation in CMIP6: Wild et al. (2021), Remote Sensing 13(21), 4464 — validation of CMIP6 rlus against ground and satellite observations; documents which models participated and their biases. ESGF data holdings for CMIP6 ScenarioMIP — authoritative overview of what is archived per experiment.

Radiation Bias Correction Guide

Background: downwelling vs net radiation

Surface radiation comes in two forms relevant for bias correction:

Downwelling radiation is what the atmosphere delivers to the surface — shortwave (ssrd / rsds) from the sun and longwave (strd / rlds) from greenhouse gases and clouds.

Net radiation is what the surface actually exchanges after reflection and re-emission. For shortwave this is ssr = ssrd − reflected SW; for longwave str = strd − surface upwelling LW. Net radiation is the physically relevant quantity for the ocean heat balance.

River Flow and Nutrient Load Projection Guide Overview This guide describes how to project future river flows and nutrient loadings using the delta-change method: bias-corrected gridded precipitation (P) and evapotranspiration (E) from CMIP6 are used to scale the observed seasonal discharge cycle from the EMORID dataset forward in time. Nutrient loads are then computed by multiplying projected discharge by an observed flow-weighted mean concentration.
EMORID observed Q + nutrients │ ├─── monthly Q climatology ──────────────────────────┐ │ (calibration period) │ │ ▼ ERA5 P, E (reference) Q_future = Q_clim(month) × ΔPE(t) │ │ └─── P–E climatology ────────────────► ΔPE(t) │ (calibration period, denominator) │ │ BC future P, E (CMIP6) ▼ │ nutrient loads = Q_future × FWMC └─── future P–E ──────────────────────► (numerator) Command river-projection --config config/river_projection.yaml river-projection --config config/river_projection.yaml --scenario ssp585 river-projection --config config/river_projection.yaml --dry-run Config on GitHub: config/river_projection.yaml
Input data Dataset Location Content EMORID observed flows /data/EMORID/to_netcdf/EMORID_1990_2024.nc 446 stations, daily Q + nutrients, 1990–2024 ERA5 precipitation /data/ERA5/kaj/era5_tp_{year}.nc Hourly accumulation (m), variable tp — reference P–E climatology ERA5 evaporation /data/ERA5/kaj/era5_e_{year}.nc Hourly accumulation (m), variable e, negative for upward flux BC future P from bc correct pipeline Daily, one file per year, variable pr (kg m⁻² s⁻¹) BC future E from bc correct pipeline Daily, one file per year, variable evspsbl (kg m⁻² s⁻¹) EMORID variables available: Q, TotalN, NO3, NH4, DIN, TotalP, PO4, Si, DOC, DIC, SPM. Countries covered: 18 (NE Atlantic and Baltic rim).
Method Step 1 — Station filtering Stations are filtered to retain only those with ≥ min_years (default 10) of valid daily Q. This removes gauges with very short or mostly-missing records that would produce unreliable climatologies.
Stations in Norway, Finland, Sweden, and Iceland are flagged with regulated_flag = True in the output but are not excluded. Rivers in these countries are heavily influenced by snowmelt timing and hydropower operations, so the P–E relationship does not hold as reliably as for rainfall-dominated catchments. Projections for flagged stations should be interpreted with caution.
Step 2 — Associate stations with gridded P–E Each station is paired with the nearest grid cell in the ERA5 P and E fields (for the climatology) and the bias-corrected future P and E fields (for the projection) using nearest-neighbour selection on (lat, lon).
This is a practical approximation: in the absence of catchment boundary polygons the point extraction captures the local climate signal but not the integrated catchment response. A future improvement would ingest catchment boundary polygons (e.g. HydroSHEDS) and compute proper area-weighted P–E over each drainage basin. The delta-change method is robust to this approximation because it relies only on the relative change in P–E, not its absolute value.
Step 3 — Monthly P–E climatology (calibration period) The mean monthly P–E at each station is computed over the calibration period (e.g. 1990–2019) from ERA5, which is the observational reference:
PE_ref_clim(m) = mean over t in calibration period of [P_ERA5(t) - E_ERA5(t)] where month(t) = m This 12-value climatology forms the denominator of the delta-change factor.
Why ERA5 and not bias-corrected historical? The BC future files are anchored to the ERA5 climatological distribution by construction. Dividing the future P–E by the ERA5 climatology therefore recovers the pure CMIP6 climate-change signal. Bias-corrected historical files would give the same result (ERA5 ≈ BC-historical by construction) and are not needed.
ERA5 sign convention: ERA5 e is stored as a negative accumulation (upward flux = surface loss). The pipeline applies scale_factor: -1 to convert it to a positive evaporation value matching CMIP6 evspsbl. ERA5 hourly files are resampled to daily totals (resample: daily) before the climatology is computed so the temporal resolution matches the BC future.
Step 4 — Observed monthly Q climatology (calibration period) The mean monthly observed discharge at each station is computed from EMORID over the same calibration period:
Q_clim(m) = mean over t in calibration period of Q_obs(t) where month(t) = m This is the baseline seasonal cycle that the delta-change method preserves.
Step 5 — Delta-change factor For each future time step t the delta-change factor is:
ΔPE(t) = PE_future(t) / PE_hist_clim(month(t)) where PE_future(t) = P_future(t) − E_future(t) from the bias-corrected future forcing.
Interpretation: ΔPE(t) = 1.2 means the future P–E in that month is 20 % higher than the historical climatological value, so the projected discharge is also 20 % higher.
Handling non-positive P–E: In arid regions or summer months, the historical P–E climatology can be zero or negative (evaporation exceeds precipitation). Division by a non-positive value is physically undefined for this method; those cells are set to ΔPE = 1.0 (no change) and logged as warnings. If many stations trigger this condition, consider extending the calibration period or reviewing the evaporation variable.
Step 6 — Projected river discharge Q_future(t) = Q_clim(month(t)) × ΔPE(t) Values are clipped to zero (no negative discharge).
Why delta-change rather than a full rainfall-runoff model? The delta-change method preserves the observed seasonal cycle and inter-annual variability pattern of discharge without requiring catchment area, soil parameters, or routing. It applies only the climate-driven fractional change in water availability (P–E) to the observed flow. The method is widely used for first-order assessments when catchment characteristics are not available.
Step 7 — Flow-weighted mean concentration (FWMC) For each nutrient species and station:
FWMC = Σ(Q(t) × C(t)) / Σ(Q(t)) over the FWMC period The FWMC period is configured independently from the calibration period. Use a recent sub-period (e.g. 2010–2023) rather than the full record to reflect current nutrient conditions; older data from the 1990s may overestimate future loads in rivers that have improved under EU nutrient directives such as the Water Framework Directive and Nitrates Directive.
Both Q and C must be finite for a time step to enter the sum. Stations with no valid Q–C overlap for a given nutrient receive FWMC = NaN and are excluded from load projections for that nutrient.
Step 8 — Projected nutrient loads Load_future(t) = Q_future(t) × FWMC [kg s⁻¹ if C in kg m⁻³] This assumes in-catchment nutrient sources and in-stream processing remain at their current level. This is a conservative baseline; a separate scenario analysis can relax it by applying trend-based or policy-driven adjustments to FWMC.
Configuration # config/river_projection.yaml metadata: scenario: ssp245 # override at run time with --scenario emorid: path: "/data/EMORID/to_netcdf/EMORID_1990_2024.nc" station_filter: min_years: 10 # minimum years of valid daily Q calibration: start: "1990-01-01" end: "2019-12-31" future: start: "2020-01-01" end: "2099-12-31" fwmc: # period for flow-weighted mean concentrations start: "2010-01-01" # omit this block to use the full EMORID record end: "2023-12-31" nutrients: - TotalN - TotalP - DIN domain: lon_min: -20.0 lon_max: 40.0 lat_min: 45.0 lat_max: 75.0 forcing: # ERA5: denominator of the delta-change factor (P–E climatology). # ERA5 e is a negative hourly accumulation → negate and resample to daily. reference: pr: path: "/data/ERA5/kaj/era5_tp_{year}.nc" variable: tp resample: daily # hourly → daily sum evspsbl: path: "/data/ERA5/kaj/era5_e_{year}.nc" variable: e scale_factor: -1 # ERA5 e is negative; negate → positive E resample: daily # BC future: numerator of the delta-change factor. future: pr: path: "/data/BiasCorrected/MPI-ESM1-2-HR/{scenario}/pr_bc_qdm_{scenario}_{year}.nc" variable: pr evspsbl: path: "/data/BiasCorrected/MPI-ESM1-2-HR/{scenario}/evspsbl_bc_qdm_{scenario}_{year}.nc" variable: evspsbl output: dir: "/data/RiverProjections/{scenario}" compress: true {scenario} in future forcing paths and the output directory is substituted at runtime from metadata.scenario (or --scenario). This makes it straightforward to run multiple SSPs:
for scenario in ssp245 ssp370 ssp585; do river-projection --config config/river_projection.yaml --scenario $scenario done Output Two NetCDF files are written per run, both with dimensions (site, time):
File Variables Description river_flows_future_<scenario>.nc Q, regulated_flag Projected monthly discharge (m³ s⁻¹) nutrient_loads_future_<scenario>.nc TotalN, TotalP, DIN, … Projected loads (kg s⁻¹) Global attributes record the scenario name, calibration and future periods, and the FWMC period.
Dry run Use --dry-run to validate the full pipeline (loading, filtering, P–E extraction, delta-change, FWMC) without writing any output files:
river-projection --config config/river_projection.yaml --dry-run The dry run logs the projected Q shape and the number of stations with valid load estimates per nutrient — useful for verifying that forcing paths resolve correctly before committing to a long multi-scenario run.
Limitations and future improvements Limitation Impact Potential improvement Nearest-neighbour P–E extraction Ignores catchment shape; grid noise can affect individual stations Ingest HydroSHEDS catchment polygons and compute area-weighted P–E Constant FWMC Does not capture land-use change or emission trends Fit a trend to historical C(t) and extrapolate; or apply emission scenario adjustments Regulated rivers flagged but not excluded Delta-change projections less physically meaningful for snowmelt / hydropower dominated basins Separate treatment using temperature-based snowmelt model No uncertainty from FWMC Single FWMC value per station Bootstrap confidence intervals on FWMC from the observed record Key references Arnell (1999), Climate change and global water resources, Global Environmental Change 9, S31–S49. — Foundation of the delta-change approach for river flow projections. Middelkoop et al. (2001), Impact of climate change on hydrological regimes and water resources management in the Rhine basin, Climatic Change 49, 105–128. — Applied delta-change example. Kronvang et al. (2009), Nitrogen and phosphorus losses from agricultural areas in European river basins, Science of the Total Environment. — FWMC methodology context for riverine nutrient loads.

EMORID observed Q + nutrients

        │    (calibration period)                            │
